coredata.dc:title: Nano-engineered carbon dots from banana blossoms inhibit advanced glycation end products formation in bakery foods: A mechanistic study through multi-stage kinetic modeling

coredata.dc:description: Banana blossoms, a natural and sustainable source, were successfully prepared to produce carbon dots (BB-CDs) with promising anti-glycation properties. Advanced glycation end products (AGEs), implicated in various degenerative diseases, were significantly reduced in food models fortified with the BB-CDs. Spherical in shape and measuring 2.06 nm, the BB-CDs possessed a rich surface chemistry, brimming with hydrophilic and reactive functional groups. The multi-stage kinetic analysis results revealed that BB-CDs inhibited the formation and accumulation of AGEs by reducing the conversion of reaction substrates to intermediates and intermediates into end products, while simultaneously enhancing the degradation of end products. In practical application, sponge cakes fortified with 2.5 g/100 g BB-CDs exhibited marked reductions in AGEs, including N ε-carboxymethyllysine and N ε-carboxyethyllysine, by over 40%, both in free and protein-bound forms. Concurrently, BB-CDs improved the moisture retention and texture of sponge cakes, softening their structure. These findings demonstrate that BB-CDs is a novel exogenous inhibitor of AGEs, which can be applied to mitigate the production of harmful substances during thermal processing of food.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Carbon dots (CDs) represent emerging zero-dimensional nanomaterials characterized by high biocompatibility, water solubility, thermal stability, and photostability (Pandey & Raikwar, 2024). These properties all support the stable and uniform dispersion of CDs in food systems during thermal processing, Furthermore inhibiting the generation of AGEs. Carbon dots derived from fruit wastes, such as cumin seeds and pineapple peels, demonstrate exceptional antioxidant activity comparable to that of polyphenols (Bayat et al., 2019; Surendran et al., 2021). Furthermore, CDs sourced from papaya leaves and pomegranate peels exhibit notable antibacterial and anti-inflammatory activities (Gudimella et al., 2022; Sattariazar et al., 2023). Additionally, CDs obtained from barley bran show significant inhibitory activity against advanced glycation end products (AGEs) (Zhu et al., 2022). Despite the established inhibitory effects of CDs on AGEs, their efficacy concerning AGEs in thermally processed foods remains unexplored. The formation of AGEs entails various intermediates, and the mechanisms by which CDs inhibit the formation of different products remain unclear. Given that banana blossoms, as a by-product of banana fruit, contain diverse antioxidant compounds such as polyphenols, polysaccharides and dietary fiber (Sheng et al., 2016, 2017; Wang et al., 2023). These ingredients have also been proven to exhibit excellent AGEs inhibitory capacity. banana blossom-derived carbon dots (BB-CDs) may present a novel and effective inhibitor of AGEs.This work was supported by the Innovational Fund for Scientific and Technological Personnel of Hainan Province (Grant No. KJRC2023C26), Natural Science Foundation of Hainan Province (NO. 324QN321), The Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences for Science and Technology Innovation Team of National Tropical Agricultural Science Center (Grant No. CATASCXTD202304). Furthermore, the authors sincerely thank the from the First Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST, Doctoral Student Special Plan.

This work was supported by the Innovational Fund for Scientific and Technological Personnel of Hainan Province (Grant No. KJRC2023C26), Natural Science Foundation of Hainan Province (NO. 324QN321), Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences for Science and Technology Innovation Team of National Tropical Agricultural Science Center (Grant No. CATASCXTD202304).

head.ce:title: Nano-engineered carbon dots from banana blossoms inhibit advanced glycation end products formation in bakery foods: A mechanistic study through multi-stage kinetic modeling

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Banana blossoms, a natural and sustainable source, were successfully prepared to produce carbon dots (BB-CDs) with promising anti-glycation properties. Advanced glycation end products (AGEs), implicated in various degenerative diseases, were significantly reduced in food models fortified with the BB-CDs. Spherical in shape and measuring 2.06 nm, the BB-CDs possessed a rich surface chemistry, brimming with hydrophilic and reactive functional groups. The multi-stage kinetic analysis results revealed that BB-CDs inhibited the formation and accumulation of AGEs by reducing the conversion of reaction substrates to intermediates and intermediates into end products, while simultaneously enhancing the degradation of end products. In practical application, sponge cakes fortified with 2.5 g/100 g BB-CDs exhibited marked reductions in AGEs, including N ε-carboxymethyllysine and N ε-carboxyethyllysine, by over 40%, both in free and protein-bound forms. Concurrently, BB-CDs improved the moisture retention and texture of sponge cakes, softening their structure. These findings demonstrate that BB-CDs is a novel exogenous inhibitor of AGEs, which can be applied to mitigate the production of harmful substances during thermal processing of food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced glycation end products (AGEs) represent hazardous by-products formed during the Maillard reaction. Studies demonstrate that AGEs' accumulation in the body increases the risk of diabetes, kidney disease, atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and other degenerative conditions (Fallavena et al., 2022). AGEs, as glycation products, result from the reaction between the carbonyl groups of reducing sugars and the free amino groups of macromolecules, including proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids. The formation of AGEs primarily consists of three stages: (1) Generation of Schiff bases; (2) Formation of Amadori rearrangement products; (3) The combination of dicarbonyl compounds, glyoxal (GO) and methylglyoxal (MGO), with Amadori products to form AGEs (Yang et al., 2024). N ε-carboxymethyllysine (CML) and N ε-carboxyethyllysine (CEL) serve as representative substances of AGEs (Wei et al., 2018). AGEs categorize into endogenous and exogenous based on their sources. Endogenous AGEs gradually accumulate within the body through normal physiological metabolic processes, while exogenous AGEs primarily enter through food intake, particularly thermally processed foods (Nowotny et al., 2018). Bakery foods rank among the most widely consumed thermally processed items, rich in sugars, proteins, and lipids-all major substrates for AGEs formation. Consequently, the exploration of methods for AGEs reduction and control in bakery foods has emerged as a prominent focus in the food safety domain.

Recent years have witnessed heightened attention on the screening of exogenous AGEs inhibitors from natural compounds. For instance, polyphenols and polysaccharides isolated from banana blossoms exhibit the ability to mitigate AGEs formation by inhibiting the non-enzymatic glycation of proteins (Sheng et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2023). Plant polyphenols, recognized as the most extensively studied exogenous AGEs inhibitors, present challenges due to partial insolubility and susceptibility to thermal degradation during food processing. Furthermore, they often involve lengthy preparation times, cumbersome handling procedures, and residual organic solvents (Sheng et al., 2016). Hence, the identification of novel exogenous inhibitors becomes imperative to ensure effective AGEs reduction and control.

Recently, certain nanomaterials have demonstrated notable AGEs inhibitory activity. For example, selenium nanoparticles have shown the capacity to diminish glycation sites on proteins, while zinc oxide nanoparticles effectively reduce the binding of protein residues to reducing sugars, subsequently decreasing AGEs formation (Ashraf et al., 2018; Du et al., 2020). The size of nanomaterials is extremely small, typically ranging from 1 to 100 nm, and their shape is relatively regular, which endows nanomaterials with excellent bioavailability and biocompatibility. In addition, nanomaterials can also serve as carriers for delivering and slow-release active substances, thereby further enhancing the inhibitory effect on AGEs (Joshi et al., 2024).

Carbon dots (CDs) represent emerging zero-dimensional nanomaterials characterized by high biocompatibility, water solubility, thermal stability, and photostability (Pandey & Raikwar, 2024). These properties all support the stable and uniform dispersion of CDs in food systems during thermal processing, Furthermore inhibiting the generation of AGEs. Carbon dots derived from fruit wastes, such as cumin seeds and pineapple peels, demonstrate exceptional antioxidant activity comparable to that of polyphenols (Bayat et al., 2019; Surendran et al., 2021). Furthermore, CDs sourced from papaya leaves and pomegranate peels exhibit notable antibacterial and anti-inflammatory activities (Gudimella et al., 2022; Sattariazar et al., 2023). Additionally, CDs obtained from barley bran show significant inhibitory activity against advanced glycation end products (AGEs) (Zhu et al., 2022). Despite the established inhibitory effects of CDs on AGEs, their efficacy concerning AGEs in thermally processed foods remains unexplored. The formation of AGEs entails various intermediates, and the mechanisms by which CDs inhibit the formation of different products remain unclear. Given that banana blossoms, as a by-product of banana fruit, contain diverse antioxidant compounds such as polyphenols, polysaccharides and dietary fiber (Sheng et al., 2016, 2017; Wang et al., 2023). These ingredients have also been proven to exhibit excellent AGEs inhibitory capacity. banana blossom-derived carbon dots (BB-CDs) may present a novel and effective inhibitor of AGEs.

In this study, BB-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method and subsequently characterized for micro-morphology, particle size distribution, elemental composition, surface structure, and optical properties. The impact of BB-CDs' addition on the formation of fluorescent and non-fluorescent AGEs was evaluated using a classical chemical model (glucose-lysine). The analysis of pathways by which BB-CDs inhibit AGEs formation was conducted through the application of a multi-stage kinetic model, designed for the description of both formation and degradation processes of reaction products across eight distinct stages (Fig. 1 ). Finally, BB-CDs were incorporated into sponge cakes to investigate their inhibitory effects on AGEs in food products and to assess their influence on the quality attributes of sponge cakes. The results of this study contribute to addressing the environmental challenges posed by banana blossom waste, facilitating the high-value recycling of waste resources, and minimizing the formation and accumulation of AGEs in bakery foods, thereby enhancing food safety.

Banana blossoms (Bao Dao) were sourced from the Hainan Tropical Fruit Tree Experimental Base, China. Quinine sulfate, phosphate buffered saline (PBS), D-glucose (Glu), L-lysine (Lys), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glyoxal (GO, 40% in water) and methylglyoxal (MGO, 40% in water) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fructosyl-lysine (FL, ≥98%) was supplied by MCE Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (New Jersey, America). CML and CEL were provided by Toronto Research Chemicals Inc. (North York, Ontario, Canada).

BB-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method (Zhu et al., 2022). Fresh banana blossoms were freeze-dried, ground into powder (7 g), and suspended in Milli-Q water (70 mL). The mixture was transferred to a 100 mL PTFE-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h (JGF-100mL, Jieang, Shanghai China). The hydrothermal product filtered, centrifuged (15294×g, 20 min), and passed through a 0.22-μm nylon membrane. The filtrate was dialyzed (1000 Da membrane) for 48 h, followed by freeze-drying to yield tan BB-CDs crystals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and particle size distribution of BB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and particle size of BB-CDs were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 20, Thermo, USA) and dynamic light scattering (DLS, ZS 920, Anton Paar, Austria). Zeta potential was measured to assess dispersion stability. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Edge, Bruker, Germany) was used to determine aggregate size distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure and elemental composition of BB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany) was performed to evaluate the crystalline structure. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, iS10, Nicolet, USA) was employed to identify functional groups, and elemental composition was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab 250Xi, Thermo, USA).

Fluorescence spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK), with the quantum yield determined. Fluorescence lifetime was measured via biexponential fitting. UV–visible spectra were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-3900, HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan).

The QYs of BB-CDs was determined following the method of Kumar et al. (2017), with minor modifications. Using quinine sulfate (QY = 54%) as a reference, excitation was set at 460 nm. The QYs was calculated using the equation:
(1)

where Q is the quantum yield, I is the fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance, and η is the refractive index.

Glu-Lys chemical models were prepared by dissolving Glu and Lys in Milli-Q water, with each solution (2 mL) added to pressure-resistant tubes. BB-CDs were introduced at various concentrations (0, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00, 1.50, 2.00, 2.50 g/100 mL), and the mixtures were heated at 160 °C for 25 min. Samples were cooled and stored at 4 °C.

For kinetic analysis, Glu (3 mL) and Lys (3 mL) were mixed with BB-CDs (1.5 g/100 mL) and heated for 60 min at temperatures increasing from 80 °C to 160 °C. Blank controls lacked BB-CDs. Samples were stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of total fluorescent AGEs and pentosidine
ce:section.ce:para: After heating the Glu-Lys models, fluorescence intensity was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan). The excitation/emission wavelengths for total fluorescent AGEs and pentosidine were at 325/440 and 335/382 nm, respectively (Huang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of non-fluorescent AGEs and reaction intermediates in multi-stage kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The concentrations of reaction substrates (Glu, Lys), intermediates (GO, MGO, FL) and end products (CML, CEL) were determined by UPLC-MS/MS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample pre-treatment and analysis of CML and CEL
ce:section.ce:para: Sample pre-treatment was required for the determination of CML and CEL in chemical models (Chen et al., 2019). The sample was passed through a 0.22-μm filter membrane, and filtrate (1 mL) was passed through a SPE column (Cleanert PCX, 150 mg/6 mL). After discarding the waste, the column was washed with 3 mL of Milli-Q water, and the eluent was collected. UPLC-MS/MS analysis was performed on a Triple Quad system (6500 + series, AB Sciex) using an X-Bridege C18 (4.6 mm × 150 mm, 5 μm) column. The mobile phases were 0.1% (V/V) aqueous formic acid (A) and acetonitrile (B), at a flow rate of 0.15 mL/min. The injection volume was 2 μL, and the column temperature was maintained at 30 °C. The gradient elution program was as follows: 2% B, 0–4.0 min, 2–60% B, 4.0–4.2 min, 60-2% B, 4.2–4.5 min, 2% B, 4.5–6.0 min. Detection was performed in positive ion mode with an ESI source in multi-reaction monitoring (MRM) mode. The qualitative and quantitative ion pair information was as follows: CML: m/z 205.0–84.2∗, 205.0–130.3; CEL: m/z 219.0–84.2∗, 219.0–130.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Derivatization and analysis of GO and MGO
ce:section.ce:para: GO and MGO required derivatization prior to UPLC-MS/MS (Wang et al., 2019) The sample (1 mL) was mixed with 0.2 g/100 mL OPD (0.6 mL) and reacted for 12 h in the dark at room temperature. After filtration, the derivatized products were analyzed using the same system. The mobile phases were 0.1% (V/V) aqueous formic acid (A) and 0.1% (V/V) formic acid in methanol (B), with a flow rate of 0.20 mL/min. The gradient elution program was as follows: 40% B, 0–4.0 min, 40–70% B, 4.0–6.0 min, 70-40% B, 6.0–6.1 min, 40% B, 6.1–8.0 min. The qualitative and quantitative ion pair were as follows: GO: m/z 131.0–77.0∗, 131.0–104.0; MGO: m/z 145.0–77.0∗, 145.0–65.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample pre-treatment and analysis of FL, Glu, and Lys
ce:section.ce:para: FL followed the same sample pre-treatment as CML and CEL. The mobile phases were 0.1% (V/V) aqueous formic acid (A) and methanol (B), with a flow rate of 0.15 mL/min. The gradient elution program was 5% B isocratic elution for 5 min. The qualitative and quantitative ion pair were as follows: FL: m/z 309.0–84.0∗, 309.0–128.3. Glu and Lys were analyzed similarly to FL, with Glu detected in negative ion mode. The qualitative and quantitative ion pair were as follows: Glu: m/z 178.8–88.9∗, 178.8–58.9; Lys: m/z 147.4–84.1∗, 147.4–130.1. ESI parameters are detailed in Table S1, with chromatograms of the reactants provided in Fig. S1. Concentrations were determined using external standard calibration curves (Fig. S2). Data analysis was performed with Agilent systems (UPLC-MS/MS solution version 3.41).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the multi-stage kinetic model and calculation of kinetic parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
A multi-stage kinetic model was developed to describe the formation and degradation of reaction products across eight key stages. The kinetic rate constants (k) were defined as follows:
k 1: Formation of FL from Glu and Lys
k 2: Formation of GO from Glu
k 3: Formation of MGO from Glu
k 4: Formation of CML from FL
k 5: Formation of CML from GO and Lys
k 6: Formation of CEL from MGO and Lys
k 7: Degradation or conversion of CML to other products
k 8: Degradation or conversion of CEL to other products
Experimental groups with BB-CDs were represented by the modified constants k 1 '–k 8 '.

The Arrhenius equation was employed to validate the kinetic model:
(2)
k = A e
‐ E a / R T
where k is the reaction rate constant, A is the preexponential factor, Ea is the empirical activation energy, R is the molar gas constant (8.314J/mol·K), and T is the Kelvin temperature.

The kinetic parameters (k 1-k 8) for each reaction stage were obtained by the following kinetic differential equations, using nonlinear fitting with Athena Visual Studio (v.14.2):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of sponge cake with added BB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Reference to reported method with minor modifications (Gan et al., 2022). BB-CDs was added into low-gluten flour at concentrations of 0, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00, 1.50, 2.00, and 2.50 (g/100 g). For each cake, 65 g of flour was used, along with 60 g of eggs, 60 g of sugar, 35 g of milk, and 20 g of butter. Egg whites were beaten, with sugar added in three portions until firm peaks formed. The milk and butter mixture was emulsified and incorporated into the whipped egg mixture. Sifted flour, mixed with BB-CDs, was gently folded into the batter. The mixture was poured into molds and baked at 150 °C for 35 min (DK-TO02, Xinbao, Guangzhou, China). The sponge cakes were stored at - 20 °C until analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AGEs and protein oxidation products in the sponge cake
Extraction and quantification of free CML and CEL
ce:section.ce:para: Free CML and CEL were extracted following the reported method (Uribarri et al., 2010). Sponge cake particles (4 g) were mixed with 50 mL PBS buffer (pH 7.0) and centrifuged at 2803×g for 20 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane, followed by solid-phase extraction as described in the chemical model section. Quantification was performed using UPLC-MS/MS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction and quantification of protein-bound CML and CEL
ce:section.ce:para: Protein-bound CML and CEL were extracted following the reported method (Assar et al., 2009). The pellet remaining after free CML and CEL extraction was treated with NaBH4 and Na2B4O7 solutions and reduced for 8 h at 4 °C, protected from light. After reduction, chloroform and methanol (2:1) were added, and the mixture was centrifuged at 18945×g for 10 min. The precipitate was hydrolyzed with concentrated HCl under nitrogen at 110 °C for 24 h. The hydrolysate was purified by solid-phase extraction and analyzed by UPLC-MS/MS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction and quantification of fluorescent AGEs and protein oxidation products
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent AGEs and protein oxidation products were determined according to Wang et al. (2022). Sponge cake particles (750 mg) were extracted using a buffer containing 0.05 mL/100 mL Tween 20, 1 mL/100 mL SDS, 5 mL/100 mL β-mercaptoethanol, and 50 mmol/L Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) for 12 h at room temperature. After centrifugation at 11210×g for 20 min, the supernatant was used to measure fluorescence intensity. The excitation/emission wavelengths for protein oxidation products DT, KYN, and N′-F-KYN were set to 330/415 nm, 365/480 nm, and 325/434 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of color, hardness and moisture content of sponge cake

ce:section.ce:para:
Surface color was performed with a colorimeter (CR-400, Konica Minolta, Tokyo, Japan). The colorimetric index E was calculated by the following formula:

where L∗ is the lightness index (0 = black, 100 = white); and a∗ (a∗+ = redness, a∗- = greenness) and b∗ (b∗+ = yellowness, b∗- = blueness) are color indexes.

The hardness was determined by a Texture Analyzer (CT3 10 K, Brookfield, Middleboro, MA) at three different locations on the surface of the sponge cake. The P/2 probe was selected, with a test distance of 10.0 mm, a measuring speed of 1.0 mm/s, and a trigger pressure of 0.05 N.

Moisture content was determined by oven-drying method. Sponge cake samples were placed in pre-dried dishes and dried at 130 °C until a constant weight was reached. The moisture content (g/100 g) was calculated using the equation:

All experiments were performed in triplicate, and data were analyzed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical significance was assessed using SPSS 24.0 (IBM, Armonk, NY). Data analysis and figure preparation were performed with Origin 2023 (Origin Lab, Northampton, USA).

BB-CDs exhibit a well-dispersed, nearly spherical morphology, with an average size of approximately 2–3 nm (Fig. 2 a). The confirmation of this narrow size distribution, as illustrated by the inset histogram, is essential for the maintenance of a high surface-area-to-volume ratio. The consistency in particle size ensures uniformity in interactions with reactive substances, thereby enhancing their effectiveness in food applications. The confirmation of particle size through dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurementsreveals a hydrodynamic diameter of approximately 40 nm, which exceeds the size observed in TEM (Fig. 2b). This discrepancy is attributed to the formation of a hydration layer and the presence of surface functional groups in solution (Filippov et al., 2023). The polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.256 indicates homogeneity within the particle population, reflecting stability in the colloidal system. Stability, in turn, is crucial for the integration of BB-CDs into food systems or pharmaceutical formulations, where uniformity in particle behavior ensures consistency in performance and reduces the risk of aggregation.

The Zeta potential measurement of −18.9 mVprovides insights into surface charge and dispersion stability (Fig. 2c). Despite the moderate charge, the potential is sufficient to prevent significant aggregation in most conditions, suggesting the likelihood of maintaining stable colloidal dispersions. Such stability is crucial for long-term efficacy in practical applications. Furthermore, the negative surface charge influences interactions with biological molecules like proteins or enzymes, which is significant for the inhibition of glycation reactions (Allen et al., 2017).

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis presents a three-dimensional representation of the BB-CDs, revealing nanoscale topography (Fig. 2d). The maximum height of approximately 6.9 nm, combined with the relatively smooth surface profile, further enhances the surface-area-to-volume ratio. This optimized surface area facilitates maximization of interactions with target molecules, such as reactive dicarbonyl compounds or proteins.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of BB-CDs displayed a broad peak at 2θ = 23.2°, suggesting an amorphous structure (Fig. 3 a). The absence of sharp diffraction peaks suggests a lack of crystallinity in the BB-CDs, consistent with carbon dots derived from natural precursors. The amorphous nature results from the accumulation of functional groups on the particle surface, hindering the development of an ordered crystalline lattice (Hui, 2023; Siddique et al., 2018). This observation is consistent with the morphology revealed by TEM, where the absence of a fixed crystalline form is attributed to the accumulation of functional groups on the particle surface. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrogram was shown in Fig. 3b. The broad peak at 3409 cm−1 corresponds to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, while the peaks at 2921 cm−1, 1597 cm−1, and 1403 cm−1 are attributed to C-H, C=O, and C-N stretches, respectively. The signal at 1111 cm−1 is assigned to C-O-C stretching, consistent with previous studies (Cong et al., 2019; Pospieszny et al., 2014).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra confirmed the elemental composition of BB-CDs, showing major contributions from carbon (66.8%), oxygen (27.7%), and nitrogen (5.5%) (Fig. 3c). High-resolution XPS spectra revealed specific bonding environments. The C1s spectrum showed peaks at 284.8 eV (C-C), 286.0 eV (C-O-C), and 288.5 eV (C=O) (Fig. 3d), while the O1s spectrum exhibited signals at 531.1 eV (C-O-C/O-H) and 532.2 eV (C=O) (Fig. 3e). The N1s spectrum presented peaks at 399.8 eV (N-H) and 400.3 eV (C-N) (Fig. 3f), consistent with prior reports (Pan et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016). These nitrogen species may have originated from amino groups in the banana blossom or from proteins and other nitrogen-rich compounds present in the precursor.

The agreement between the XPS and FTIR data provides confirmation of the decoration of BB-CDs with various surface functional groups, including hydroxyl (O-H), carbonyl (C=O), amine (N-H), and ether (C-O-C) groups. This functionalization enhances water solubility while also increasing chemical reactivity and bioactivity, rendering the BB-CDs suitable for biomedical and food system applications. The presence of O-H and C=O groups, in particular, facilitates biocompatibility and enables interactions with biological molecules, increasing the potential for BB-CDs to function as carriers of bioactive compounds or as inhibitors of AGEs in food matrices. Additionally, the inclusion of nitrogen-containing groups, such as C-N and N-H, strengthens the potential for protein and enzyme interaction, further expanding the application possibilities for BB-CDs (Zhu et al., 2022).

The fluorescence spectrum of BB-CDs revealed a maximum excitation/emission (Ex/Em) pair at 370/460 nm (Fig. 4 a), confirming the bright luminescence characteristic of carbon dots (Lu et al., 2015). This photoluminescence supports the successful synthesis of BB-CDs. Fluorescence lifetime (τ) measurements, shown in the decay curve (Fig. 4b), provided a τ value of 5.31 ns, derived through biexponential fitting (Table S2). The fluorescence lifetime reflects the time taken for the excited fluorophores to return to the ground state, with shorter τ values indicating higher photon emission rates. This short lifetime suggests high luminescence efficiency for BB-CDs. The Quantum Yield (Qys) of BB-CDs was measured at 8.8%, consistent with QYs reported for carbon dots synthesized from other sources (Kumari et al., 2018; Surendran et al., 2021), further demonstrating the strong luminescent properties of the BB-CDs. Meanwhile, we found that the yield of BB-CDs was also relatively excellent, at 7.62%. Additionally, UV–visible spectroscopy showed a maximum absorption at 270 nm (Fig. 4c), attributed to π-π∗ transitions, characteristic of the electronic structure of carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of total fluorescent AGEs and pentosidine by BB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of varying BB-CDs concentrations on the formation of total fluorescent AGEs and pentosidine is shown in Fig. 4d. A dose-dependent decrease was observed, with significant reductions (P < 0.05) at a BB-CDs addition of 0.50 g/100 mL. At 2.50 g/100 mL BB-CDs, the inhibition rates for total fluorescent AGEs and pentosidine were 58.63% and 58.60%, respectively, though differences between these groups were not statistically significant (P > 0.05). These findings indicate that 0.50 g/100 mL BB-CDs is the optimal concentration for inhibiting fluorescent AGEs. This indicates a potent inhibitory effect of BB-CDs, prompting further investigation into their ability to inhibit non-fluorescent AGEs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of non-fluorescent AGEs by BB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effect of BB-CDs on non-fluorescent AGEs, CML and CEL, known markers of AGEs, were quantified by UPLC-MS/MS (Fig. 4e). Increasing BB-CDs concentrations led to significant reductions in CML and CEL levels (P < 0.05). At 2.50g/100 mL BB-CDs, the inhibition rates for CML and CEL reached 98.44% and 97.59%, respectively, indicating near-complete inhibition of these AGEs.

Given that glyoxal (GO) and methylglyoxal (MGO) are key precursors for CML and CEL, their inhibition was also examined. GO and MGO are highly reactive dicarbonyl compounds, with a much greater glycation capacity than glucose (Han et al., 2013), forming CML and CEL through irreversible binding to lysine residues (Hwang et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 4f, BB-CDs significantly inhibited the formation of GO and MGO in a dose-dependent manner (P < 0.05). At 2.5 g/100 mL BB-CDs, the inhibition rates of GO and MGO were 79.72% and 90.17%, respectively, demonstrating strong suppression of these reactive precursors.

Overall, the above results indicated that BB-CDs had excellent ability to inhibit AGEs, with similar or better inhibition effects compared to other AGEs inhibitors (such as the polyphenols and polysaccharides extracted from banana blossoms) reported (Sheng et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2023). To further elucidate the pathways through which BB-CDs inhibit AGEs formation, a multi-stage kinetic model was employed for detailed mechanistic analysis.

The formation of AGEs is a multifaceted reaction involving numerous intermediates. Kinetic modeling are effective methods for analyzing the rates of both formation and degradation of AGEs at different stages. A primary kinetic model verified that glucose (Glu) is first converted into the intermediate GO, which subsequently forms the final product, CML. However, this model captures only a simplified pathway of AGEs formation. To better elucidate the complexity of AGEs formation and the inhibitory role of BB-CDs, a multi-stage kinetic model was employed. This model provides a more comprehensive representation of the reaction pathways and enables a deeper understanding of the stages at which BB-CDs effectively interfere with AGEs formation.

The kinetic trends of the reaction substrates Glu and Lys are shown in Fig. 5 a & b, respectively. In both the experimental and control groups, Glu levels exhibited a decreasing trend across all five temperatures, with faster consumption observed at higher temperatures. Notably, Glu concentrations in the experimental group were consistently higher than in the control group, indicating that BB-CDs reduced Glu consumption during the reaction.

Interestingly, the kinetic behavior of Lys mirrored that of Glu, yet Lys concentrations in the experimental group were slightly lower than in the control group. This suggests that while BB-CDs decreased Glu consumption, they enhanced Lys consumption. This effect may be attributed to the reactive carbonyl groups on the surface of BB-CDs, which can interact with amino groups (Xu et al., 2024), leading to competitive binding of Lys by BB-CDs rather than Glu.

The reduced interaction between Glu and Lys in the presence of BB-CDs resulted in lower formation of the intermediate fructosyl-lysine (FL), effectively inhibiting the formation of AGEs at the source. This demonstrates that BB-CDs can intervene early in the reaction process, reducing the conversion of substrates into intermediates critical for AGEs formation.

The kinetic trends of FL, GO and MGO are shown in Fig. 5c–e, respectively, analyzed in combination with the kinetic parameters in Tables 1 and 2
. In the control group, FL concentration increased initially and then decreased at 80–100 °C, indicating that the formation rate of FL was lower than its conversion to CML at lower temperatures over time (k 1 < k 4). This also suggests that CML could be rapidly produced at these lower temperatures. At higher temperatures (120–160 °C), FL was generated quickly within 5–10 min, followed by a gradual decline in concentration, indicating that both FL formation and its conversion to CML were accelerated under elevated temperatures. This suggests that high-temperature conditions promote CML formation.

In the experimental group, the overall rates of both FL formation and its conversion to CML decreased (k 1' < k 1, k 4' < k 4). Between 80 and 120 °C, the FL formation rate was lower than its conversion rate to CML (k 1' < k 4'), resulting in a very low FL concentration in the system. However, at higher temperatures (140–160 °C), the FL concentration in the experimental group was higher than in the control group. This is likely because, at elevated temperatures, FL formation occurs rapidly, but the presence of BB-CDs effectively inhibits its conversion to CML, causing the conversion rate (k 4') to be slower than the formation rate (k 1'), allowing FL to accumulate. These findings suggest that BB-CDs inhibit AGEs formation by slowing the conversion of intermediates like FL into end products such as CML, particularly under high-temperature conditions.

The concentration of GO in the control group increased gradually with the reaction time at 80–100 °C. At 120 °C, the concentration of GO first increased and then declined, a trend that became more pronounced at 140–160 °C. This suggests that higher temperatures accelerate the conversion of GO to CML. The kinetic behavior of MGO followed a similar pattern to GO.

In the experimental group, the concentrations of GO and MGO showed a significant decrease at all five temperatures, indicating that the conversion rates from Glu to GO and MGO, as well as from GO to CML and from MGO to CEL, were notably decreased (k 2' < k 2, k 3' < k 3, k 5' < k 5, k 6' < k 6). This suggests that BB-CDs effectively slow down the conversion of intermediates to AGEs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suppression of end-product formation and enhancement of AGEs degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetic trends for the end products, CML and CEL, are presented in Fig. 5f & g. In the control group, the concentrations of CML and CEL gradually increased over time, with higher temperatures leading to greater accumulation. Under high-temperature conditions, both CML and CEL formed and accumulated rapidly in a short period, and their trends were basically similar.

In contrast, the experimental group exhibited significant inhibition of CML and CEL formation under all conditions. The degradation rates of CML and CEL were also enhanced in the presence of BB-CDs (k 7' > k 7, k 8' > k 8). This indicates that BB-CDs not only inhibit AGEs formation by reducing the conversion rates from substrates to intermediates and from intermediates to end products, but they also promote the degradation of already-formed AGEs, limiting their accumulation. These results demonstrate that BB-CDs effectively inhibit AGEs formation throughout the reaction process and enhance the degradation of AGEs, offering a comprehensive mechanism for their anti-glycation activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of BB-CDs on the formation of AGEs and protein oxidation products in sponge cakes
ce:section.ce:para: AGEs exist in both free and protein-bound forms, depending on whether they crosslink with proteins or peptides (Uribarri et al., 2010). The effects of BB-CDs addition on free and protein-bound CML and CEL formation in sponge cake were shown in Fig. 6 a & b. Increasing BB-CDs concentrations led to a significant reduction in free CML and CEL formation (P < 0.05). At a 2.50 g/100 g BB-CDs addition, inhibition rates for free CML and CEL were 51.45% and 42.44%, respectively. Despite the complexity of the food matrix-rich in proteins, which could adsorb BB-CDs and thereby moderate their inhibitory capacity (Lei et al., 2024). BB-CDs maintained substantial efficacy in inhibiting AGEs. A similar trend was observed for protein-bound CML and CEL, with 2.50 g/100 g BB-CDs resulting in inhibition rates of 44.52% and 45.89%, respectively.

Protein oxidation products are often used as an indicator of glycation severity in foods (Gong et al., 2024). With BB-CDs addition, reductions in protein oxidation products, total fluorescent AGEs, and pentosidine were observed (Fig. 6c). At 2.50 g/100 g BB-CDs, inhibition rates of dityrosine (DT), kynurenine (KYN), N′-formylkynurenine (N′-F-KYN), total fluorescent AGEs, and pentosidine were 58.0%, 74.32%, 60.66%, 64.04%, and 60.40%, respectively. The above results indicated that BB-CDs effectively reduce protein oxidation and AGEs formation in sponge cakes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of BB-CDs on color, hardness and moisture content of sponge cakes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 presents the images of sponge cakes with different amounts of BB-CDs added and the corresponding colorimetric values are summarized in Table 3 . As BB-CDs concentration increased, the L∗ value and E value progressively decreased, reflecting a darkening of the cake surface. The decrease in E illustrated there were color differences between groups, which was related to the change in L∗. The changes in surface color were mainly attributed to the brown color properties of BB-CDs. Despite these shifts, the positive b∗ value was maintained, indicating that the characteristic golden hue of the cakes remained unaffected.

Additionally, Table 3 reveals a progressive decrease in hardness and an increase in moisture content with increasing BB-CDs levels. This is likely due to the hydrophilic nature of BB-CDs, upon addition to food systems, they can rapidly absorb moisture, enhance internal humidity, and effectively retain moisture and reduce water evaporation, thereby reducing water loss in sponge cakes, which enhances water retention (Song, Li, & Zhou, 2013). Research has shown that the higher the moisture content of sponge cake, the softer and more moist the texture becomes, and the lower its hardness. The addition of BB-CDs enhances the water retention of sponge cake, ultimately leading to a decrease in the hardness of the sponge cake (Sun et al., 2024). As a result, BB-CDs improve the palatability of sponge cakes by creating a softer, more desirable texture.

In summary, it can be observed that when the addition level of BB-CDs is 2.50 g/100 g, not only does it exhibit the best AGEs inhibition effect on sponge cake, but also results in a higher edible quality.

BB-CDs exhibited a nearly spherical morphology with a monomer size of 2.06 nm, an amorphous structure, and a surface rich in hydrophilic and reactive functional groups, contributing to excellent luminescent properties. BB-CDs significantly inhibited the formation of AGEs in both chemical models and sponge cakes. At 2.5 g/100 mL BB-CDs, the inhibition rates of CML, CEL, GO, and MGO in chemical models were 98.44%, 97.59%, 79.72%, and 90.17%, respectively, while total fluorescent AGEs and pentosidine were reduced by over 58%. The mechanism of inhibition involved reducing the conversion of substrates and intermediates to AGEs and promoting the degradation of pre-formed AGEs. In sponge cakes, BB-CDs effectively inhibited both fluorescent and non-fluorescent AGEs, reduced protein oxidation, and enhanced water retention, resulting in a softer texture. In summary, these findings position BB-CDs as a promising exogenous inhibitor of AGEs, with potential applications in improving food safety.
